QF.id: Registration, Code of Conduct and General Obligations_44

question.text: A merchant banker has a liquid net worth of Rs. 8 crore and has already committed underwriting obligations of Rs. 120 crore. What additional underwriting can it take on?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Maximum underwriting = 20 × liquid net worth = 20 × Rs. 8 crore = Rs. 160 crore. Already committed = Rs. 120 crore. Additional capacity = Rs. 160 crore − Rs. 120 crore = Rs. 40 crore. **The Trap:** Rs. 80 crore would be correct if the multiplier were 25x (Rs. 200 crore total). Rs. 120 crore assumes the existing obligations don't count — but the 20x limit applies to TOTAL obligations. **Cross-Exam Context:** Capital adequacy calculations are tested in NISM Series VIII (Equity Derivatives) for clearing members with different multiplier ratios. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Track your underwriting capacity in real-time — as liquid net worth fluctuates, your maximum permitted obligation changes dynamically.
